coredata.dc:title: Boosting bioelectricity generation in microbial fuel cells using metal@metal oxides/nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Farahmand Habibi, Maryam
Arvand, Majid
Sohrabnezhad, Shabnam
coredata.dc:description: Microbial fuel cells have recently received significant consideration from researchers worldwide as sustainable and futuristic energy due to their potential in converting energy from decomposition of natural organisms in waste to green electricity. Unfortunately, the difficulty of achieving high power due to poor extracellular electron transfer (EET) efficiency between microorganisms and the solid substrate, besides low bacterial loading capacity has limited their applications to date. Herein, iron/iron oxide (Fe@Fe2O3) nanoparticles incorporated with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) are synthesized via using an effective and simple electrodeposition technique. Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs anode provides not only a high effective surface area for the adhesion of microbe’s cells but also promotes favored electrical conductivity to facilitate EET from bacteria to the anode in the mixed culture-based MFCs. Considerably, at a steady-state of the electricity production, the MFC equipped with Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs as activated anode delivers a maximum power density of 836 ± 8 mW/m2, which is 87% higher compared to instances when NCQDs (446 ± 11 mW/m2) is applied as anode electrocatalyst. This work opens a door toward an effective route to microbial anode electrode to produce sustainable green energy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs
Microbial fuel cells
Extracellular electron transfer
Electrodeposition technique

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of EET process for different anodes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration showing the fabrication process of the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs and the use of the as-papered electrode for MFC in mixed culture media.
FESEM images of (a–a") pristine CC and (b–b") Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs at low and high magnifications.
(a) XRD patterns of CC, NCQDs, Fe@Fe2O3 and Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs; (b) TEM images of NCQDs and (c–h) EDS detecting of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs (inset of (c): EDS spectrum of NCQDs).
(a) Cyclic voltammograms and (b) Nyquist curves of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs, NCQDs and CC in 0.1 mol/L KCl containing 5 mmol/L [Fe(CN)6]3–/4–.
(a) Performance, (b) power density curve and (c) polarization curve of the dual-chamber MFCs for Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs and CC anodes (Arrows indicate replacement of the acetate medium).

Cyclic voltammograms of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs, NCQDs and CC anodes under (a) turnover condition and (b) non-turnover condition with a scan rate of 5 mV/s; Inset of (a): cyclic voltammograms of different electrodes before inoculation.
Tafel plots of the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs, NCQDs and CC anodes.
FESEM images of biofilm growing on the (a) Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs, (b) NCQDs, (c) CC anodes and (d) schematic illustration on charge transfer mechanism between bacteria and Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs anode of MFC.
Comparison of the performance of microbial fuel cell using Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs modified anode with previous literatures.

row.entry: Carbon cloth coated with 0.5 mg/cm2 Pt/C
Conductive polyaniline nanoflower modified carbon cloth
Tartaric acid doped PANI modified carbon cloth
Conductive carbon nanotube hydrogel modified carbon paper
Carbon cloth coated with 0.05 mg/cm2 Pt/C
Honeycomb-like hierarchical porous carbon–silica modified carbon cloth
Oriented TiO2 nanosheets modified carbon paper
Polyaniline and reduced graphene oxide modified carbon cloth
Polyaniline/graphene and TiO2 modified graphite sheets
Polyaniline/graphene and TiO2 modified graphite sheets
Fe/Zinc oxide nanoparticles modified carbon paper

head.ce:title: Boosting bioelectricity generation in microbial fuel cells using metal@metal oxides/nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Guilan, Namjoo Street, P.O. Box: 1914–41335, Rasht, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Guilan, Namjoo Street, P.O. Box: 1914-41335, Rasht, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microbial fuel cells have recently received significant consideration from researchers worldwide as sustainable and futuristic energy due to their potential in converting energy from decomposition of natural organisms in waste to green electricity. Unfortunately, the difficulty of achieving high power due to poor extracellular electron transfer (EET) efficiency between microorganisms and the solid substrate, besides low bacterial loading capacity has limited their applications to date. Herein, iron/iron oxide (Fe@Fe2O3) nanoparticles incorporated with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) are synthesized via using an effective and simple electrodeposition technique. Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs anode provides not only a high effective surface area for the adhesion of microbe’s cells but also promotes favored electrical conductivity to facilitate EET from bacteria to the anode in the mixed culture-based MFCs. Considerably, at a steady-state of the electricity production, the MFC equipped with Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs as activated anode delivers a maximum power density of 836 ± 8 mW/m2, which is 87% higher compared to instances when NCQDs (446 ± 11 mW/m2) is applied as anode electrocatalyst. This work opens a door toward an effective route to microbial anode electrode to produce sustainable green energy.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently population growth and modernization have extensively raised the global demand for clean energy and water. Around the world, 1.3 billion people lack access to electricity and 783 million people lacking access to clean water [1,2]. Environmental pollution and global warming caused by ever-increasing energy demand contribute to the urgency for new renewable and reliable energy sources to attain sustainable development [3,4]. Hence, this has made an urgent search for development of energy efficient and environmental friendly methods for sustainable wastewater treatment and energy production. As a potential renewable feedstock to overcome the energy crisis and environmental problems, a surplus of harvestable, sustainable, green and renewable energy is found in organic waste/wastewater [5]. Recently, microbial electrochemical technologies, including microbial fuel cells (MFCs) as a “green” energy-producing device, can directly generate electrical energy from the degradation of organic wastes to smaller molecules through using microbes respiration [6,7]. Microbes, present in the anode chamber, called anode respiring bacteria, oxidize fuel such as organic waste present in polluted wastewater before safely discharging it, generating electrons and protons. Anode respiring bacteria carry out an oxidation reaction of organic matter, producing electrons and protons and thus an electric current from biomass. Although conceptually similar to batteries (both are constituted by an anode and a cathode), fuel cells receive a continuous supply of fuel. For example, hydrogen in the case of traditional fuel cells and organic material in the case of MFC [8].

MFCs are gaining popularity as a promising tool because of their mild operating conditions, environmental significance, high resource utilization efficiency, being run at ambient temperature and atmosphere with no pollution [9]. MFCs and bio-electrochemical devices illustrate major potential in the area of environment, wastewater treatment, biosensor and bioenergy [10]. The energy generated by MFCs is expected to supply enough energy to partially cover the energy demand in urban wastewater treatment plants. In addition, such systems have characteristics that fall within the context of Circular Economy, as they allow the achievement of value-added chemical compounds, such as biomethane and biohydrogen from a various sources, including organic acids and sugars or even domestic/industrial wastes. However, MFC technology has received less investment compared to other renewable energy sources, delaying the overcoming of technological issues and their dissemination at competitive prices. Considering fuel cell technology, many improvements are needed to MFC reach the efficiency and power levels of fuel cells already consolidated, as solid oxide fuel cells, molten carbonate fuel cells and proton exchange membrane fuel cells [11–15].

However, the main bottleneck of MFCs is the low output power density owing to microorganism culture, operating temperature, internal resistance, anodic and cathodic electrocatalysts, organic substrate concentration, reactor configuration, and inadequate bacterial cells loading on the anode [16,17]. The practical application of MFCs in wastewater treatment and recovery of electricity still faces problems of low power generation and a lack of energy storage. Among these agents, electrode engineering at an accelerating anodic process is considered the active and efficient factor determining the MFCs boosting current and power generation [18]. The anode provides a carrier for bacterial cells growth, so the anode surface morphology and chemistry should possess excellent chemical stability, high conductivity, biocompatibility, super hydrophilicity, large specific surface area, low-cost and non-corrosive characteristics [19–22].

Herein, we present a facile and novel anode hybrid electrocatalyst, NCQDs decorated with Fe@Fe2O3, to increase the output power density based on the following consideration: (1) nanostructured metal@metal oxides specially Fe@Fe2O3 are effective electrode materials for surface modification in the view of the unique combination of reactivity, easy diffusion pathways, selectivity and stability [23]. In addition, many dissimilatory metal-reducing bacteria can respire using iron(III) as insoluble and extracellular substrates [24]; (2) anchoring of Fe@Fe2O3 nanoparticles over NCQDs not only improves the surface area and electrical conductivity of NCQDs but also may enrich electricity-conducting bacteria on the solid substrate and enhance extracellular electron transfer in MFCs. Consequently, the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs could start up immediately, and attain a high power density of 836 ± 8 mW/m2 and current density of 2.63 ± 0.02 A/m2 in mixed culture-based MFCs.

NCQDs solution was synthesized by a straightforward one-step hydrothermal method based on Hu et al. and Guan et al. reports [25]. In a typical synthesis process, 50 mL poly(tetra-fluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave was filled with 25 mL ethylene glycol. Afterward, the autoclave containing ethylene glycol was sealed for the aforementioned reaction for 5 h at 200 °C. Then, the obtained yellowish solution was allowed to cool down until it reached room temperature. To obtain NCQDs, 1.35 mL ethylenediamine was dropped to the aforesaid solution. After stirring at ambient temperature for 30 min, the hydrothermal reaction was done in a sealed autoclave for 5 h at 180 °C. Finally, the obtained NCQDs that were naturally cooled down to the ambient temperature were collected. To make anode electrodes, CC was chosen to act as the base electrode for the electrochemical deposition of NQCDs. For the electrodeposition, two CC served as the anodic and cathodic electrodes. Prior to application, CC was pre-cleared with acetone for 12 h and 2 mol/L HCl for 4 h. Both the CC electrodes were partially-soaked into the deposition solution containing 10 mL NCQDs, 25 mL N,N-dimethylacetamide solution and 168.5 mg of hexadecyltrimethylammonium chloride. The anode and cathode were connected to the power supply and the desirable direct current (DC) voltage at ∼30 V was applied for 10 min. After electrodeposition, the purpose electrode was washed with deionized water. Eventually, NCQDs on the surface of the CC electrode was obtained.

Fe@Fe2O3 nanoparticles catalyst film was deposited on NCQDs using direct current. The graphite rod and modified CC substrate, with the approximate distance of 2 cm, were taken as the anode (counter electrode) and the cathode (working electrode) in the deposition electrolyte solution which contained 0.2 mol/L iron(III) chloride aqueous solution, respectively. Ultimately, Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs were obtained by using DC voltage power supply through applying a voltage of 20 V for 20 min as the deposition time.

X-ray diffractometer (XRD) data of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs and NCQDs were saved by Philips PW1730 XRD generator made in the Netherlands with an X-ray source of CuK α radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) to verify the crystalline structure. To investigate the element composition and surface morphology of materials of the samples, transmission electron microscope (USA, 200 kV, JEM-2100F, JEOL, TEM), Tescan Mira III elemental mapping energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) made in the Czech Republic were applied, respectively.

In this study, the experiments were done using cubic dual chambers MFCs having an inner volumetric capacity of 50 mL with equal dimensions (4 cm
×
4.5 cm
×
5.5 cm) for each chamber. Two cuboid compartments were separated from each other using Nafion 117 (DuPont Inc., USA) as a proton exchange membrane. Prior to use, the Nafion 117 membrane was etched with 3% boiling H2O2 and treated with boiling deionized water, 0.5 mol/L boiling H2SO4 and finally rinsed in boiling deionized water again. The so-called prepared Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs and NCQDs acted as modified anodes and CC was used as the control and cathode electrodes. The anaerobic compartment (anodic chamber) of biofuel cell was inoculated with the active sludge (Bandar Anzali Wastewater Treatment Plant, Iran) to run MFCs. Inoculation of MFCs with the active sludge was controlled as 50% (v/v) of the anolyte solution. The anode medium consisted of 100 mmol/L phosphate buffer (pH = 7) contained the following ammonium chloride; sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate; disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate; potassium chloride and a trace-element solution (12.5 mL) including nicotinic acid, sodium chloride, manganese sulfate monohydrate, magnesium sulfate, ferrous sulfate heptahydrate, sodium tungstate dihydrate, sodium molybdate, boric acid, aluminum potassium sulfate dodecahydrate, zinc chloride, cobalt chloride hexahydrate, nickel chloride hexahydrate, copper sulfate pentahydrate, calcium chloride dihydrate, vitamin solution (5 mL) and sodium acetate (1 g/L) as a nutrient solution. The anaerobic compartment was continuously sparged with N2 for approximately 20 min to eliminate the dissolved oxygen. The aerobic compartment (cathodic chambers) was loaded with 50 mmol/L potassium ferricyanide and 100 mmol/L potassium phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7). The MFCs were operated in the fed-batch mode condition.

The voltage output data of MFCs was monitored from an online data logger device every 30 min. All MFCs were run in batch mode at ambient temperature with an external loading resistance (1000 Ω). When the cell voltage dropped below 50 mV, 80% of medium solution was replaced with a new solution. Finally, when the MFCs were run for one-month, electrochemical polarization plots were obtained using various external resistors over a range from 100 to 0.1 KΩ for 20 min. All MFC experiments were conducted in triplicate to ensure the reproducibility of the experimental results. The measured voltage was converted to an electric current by Ohm’s law equation:
(1)
I
(
electric current , A
)
=
V
(
output voltage , V
)
R
(
external resistance , Ω
)

The produced output power was computed based on the following equation:
(2)
P
(
output power , W
)
= V
(
output voltage , V
)
× I
( electric current , A )
To normalize the current and power output, they were optimized to anode surface area and were named power density and current density, respectively.

Other electrochemical evaluations including cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were done on Autolab PGSTAT 30 electrochemical analyzer (Eco Chemie BV, Utrecht, the Netherlands) in classical three-electrode set up. The parameters were as follows: in regard of linear polarization, start potential = −0.08 mV, stop potential = 0.08 mV, scan rate = 1 mV/s; as for EIS, first applied frequency = 0.01 Hz, last applied frequency = 100 KHz, potential amplitude = 10 mV; for CV in turnover and non-turnover situation, start potential = −0.6 mV, stop potential = 0.6 mV, scan rate = 5 mV/s. The electrochemical behaviors of electrodes were investigated in 5.0 mmol/L [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– in supporting electrolyte (KCl 0.1 mol/L) in a potential range of −0.4 to 0.8 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at a scan rate of 100 mV/s.

The facile synthetic route of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs hybrid is graphically represented in Fig. 1 . The surface morphologies of CC and Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs were probed by FESEM in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2(a−a") clearly demonstrated the morphology of the pristine CC before deposition. It can be seen that the woven carbon microfibers belongs to original CC with a smooth surface. Since the two-step deposition, the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs anchored on the surface of the microfibers and thoroughly covered the CC skeleton. FESEM examination of the hybrid sample showed a rough structural morphology due to an abundant number of nanoparticles dispersed in the CC matrix (ranging from 20 to 24 nm) and the formation of intertwined network nanoparticles on the active substance (Fig. 2(b−b")). The TEM examination was done to clarify the structure of the as-formed QDs. The TEM image of NCQDs in Fig. 3 b reveals uniform spherical morphology with an average size of estimated less than 20 nm.

The crystalline nature of the as-prepared hybrid materials was obtained by the XRD study (Fig. 3a). The XRD patterns of the pristine CC and NCQDs are in good consistent with the standard patterns for the carbon (JCPDS No. 00-041-1487), and no peaks from other crystal phases were detected. For pristine CC and NCQDs, the diffraction peak located at a 2θ value of 25 is associated with the carbon (002) plane. The XRD analysis of the Fe@Fe2O3 shows two peaks located at 2θ value of 44.6 and 65 which can be indexed to the (110) and (200) crystal planes of Fe (JCPDS No. 00-0006-0696), implying that the main composition of nanoparticles are Fe metal in the as-prepared hybrid material, and the extra peaks are ascribed to Fe2O3 (JCPDS No. 00-039-1346). All the diffraction peaks of the Fe2O3 correspond well with the standard pattern, which means the high purity of the as-prepared sample. In addition, the pattern of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs hybrid contains the diffraction peaks of both Fe@Fe2O3 and NCQDs, indicating the successfully deposition of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs coating on the CC.

The EDS elemental spot confirmed the presence of N, Fe, O and C homogeneously distributed in the detected area (Fig. 3c). Besides, EDS elemental mapping unambiguously corroborates the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs structure (Fig. 3(d−h)). It is noted that the elemental spot analysis of a single NCQDs unfolds the presence of N, O and C which was introduced during the deposition of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs hybrid nanocomposite (Fig. 3c (inset)).

The probable electrodeposition mechanism for iron oxides can be described here based on the previous reports [26–28]. In brief, the applied voltage between the two electrodes (electrodeposition cell) produces a current which led to electrolyte decomposition, releasing hydrogen and hydroxyl ions, eq. (3):
(3) FeCl3.6H2O + 6e– → 3H2(g) + 6OH− + Fe3+
(4) Fe3+ + 1e– → Fe2+
(5) Fe2+ + 2e– → Fe

Furthermore, these hydroxyl ions are expected to react with iron ions (in the electrolyte) and as a result, the iron hydroxide is deposited on the electrodes according to eq. (6):
(6) Fe2+ + 2OH− → Fe(OH)2(s)

Then the generated Fe(OH)2 is decomposed into iron oxyhydroxide (FeOOH) nucleates (eq. (7)):
(7) Fe(OH)2 + 2OH− → FeOOH + H2O + e–
(8) 2FeOOH → Fe2O3 + H2O

CC have -COOH, -OH and = O functional groups and NCQDs are including hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine and amide functional groups. After applying desirable direct current voltage and under the electric field, the particles in the suspension first migrate to one of the electrodes, depending on the particle charge, where they eventually deposit. The proposed possible reactions/bonding among of the NCQDs on CC can be related to “hydrogen bonding”. The binding force is due to hydrogen bonds between the carboxyl group of a CC and the amine group of NCQDs. For reactions/bonding among Fe@Fe2O3 and NCQDs, it seems that the applied voltage between the anode and cathode electrodes produces a current which led to the electrolyte decomposition, releasing hydrogen and hydroxyl ions (eq. (3)). When the generated Fe(OH)2 is decomposed into iron oxyhydroxide (FeOOH) nucleates (eq. (7)), the FeOOH used as the Fe source is electrostatically attracted to the NCQDs surface and based on eq. (8) is converted to Fe2O3 to form Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs layers.

The electrochemical characterizations of CC, NCQDs and Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs were studied by CV and EIS to evaluate electrode activity in order to release and transit electrons captured in the substrate to solid electrodes. The CV and EIS measurements of the prepared electrodes in a three-electrode system containing 5.0 mmol/L [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– in supporting electrolyte (KCl 0.1 mol/L) as the electrolyte were systematically conducted. The comparative CV test results of the CC, NCQDs and Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs anodes are illustrated in Fig. 4 a. The sharp redox peaks with a much higher current density visible in Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs voltammogram prove the largest surrounded area and electrical merits of the hybrid electrode. It is worth noting that the CC presents the smallest peak current density in comparison to the NCQDs and Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs due to weak electron transfer rate and low available surface area. Hence, the introduction of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs has improved the electrode charge capacity and EET kinetics between bacteria and electrode.

EIS proffers an efficient approach for the behavior of individual layers of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs by finding the conductivity and diffusion property (Fig. 4b). To further understanding the improved electrochemical performance of the as-obtained hybrid electrode, EIS analysis was employed at open circuit potential (OCP) over the frequency range of 0.01 Hz–100 kHz in 0.1 mol/L KCl containing 5 mmol/L [Fe(CN)6]3–/4–. Fig. 4b compares the Nyquist plots of the EIS spectra for the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs, NCQDs and CC electrodes; and the inset of Fig. 4b gives the equivalent circuit used to fit the EIS spectra to measure solution resistance (R s) and interfacial charge transfer resistance (R ct); where Z w and CPE are the Warburg impedance and the constant phase element, respectively. Generally, all the Nyquist plots of the EIS spectra composed of approximate semicircle at high frequency region followed by a linear component at low frequency region. At high frequency region, the real axis intercept indicates the R s of the electrochemical system and the semicircle diameter reflects the R ct, which caused by faradaic reactions. The impedance behavior at the high frequency region is characteristic of the resistance at the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− electrolyte interface due to discontinuity in the charge transfer process at the electrode/liquid electrolyte interface which is a result of difference in the conductivity between electrode (electronic conductivity) and the aqueous electrolyte phase (ionic conductivity). As can be seen in Fig. 4b, a large semicircle was exhibited by bare CC because of CC has woven carbon microfibers with a smooth surface. When the bare CC was modified with NCQDs, the R ct decreased, indicating that NCQDs is a good conductive material and accelerate the electron transfer. It revealed the satisfactory electrical conductivity of NCQDs. The curve of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs exhibited negligible semicircle which reflects its better electrochemical performance, rapid electron transport kinetics and negligible charge transfer resistance at the electrode/electrolyte interface. The reason may be that Fe@Fe2O3 and NCQDs nanomaterials provide more active sites for the electrochemical reaction of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. Furthermore, the remarkably decreased R ct for the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs showed that incorporation of Fe@Fe2O3 in the NCQDs layer could create necessary conduction pathways in promoting the electron transfer on the resultant electrode. In other words, Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs possessed the minimum electron transfer resistance and showed the best conductivity. Furthermore, the rate of an electrochemical reaction can be strongly influenced by diffusion of reactants or product at the electrode surface. The linear parts in the impedance plots at lower frequencies correspond to the Z w, which is described as a diffusive resistance of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− ion within the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs electrode pores. As shown in low-frequency region, the straight line of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs extend steeper than that of CC and NCQDs, indicating a weaker impact controlled by diffusion process, implied an excellent mass transfer process through Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs anode. Utilization of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs with higher specific surface area (with a highly porous structure) has the potential to provide more available reaction sites for electrode. CC reveals a significant change in the structural morphology from smooth to rough, owing to the deposition of the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs and the formation of an intertwined network porous nanostructure on the anode surface. Therefore, diffusive of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− ion within the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs electrode pores was increased. Together, the EIS spectra confirm that the diffusional process is facilitated at the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs, and the charge transfer resistance is decreased at a higher frequency. So, Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs modification holds great potential to improve MFC performance.

The benefits of stable electrochemical behavior, high surface coverage, supreme electronic conductivity and good biocompatibility make Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs an ideal choice for the anode electrode in MFC. To evaluate the cell performance and the bioelectricity generation of the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs, a dual-chamber MFC in a stacked configuration was fabricated. The anolyte (acetate solution) or inoculated solution (acetate solution with the anaerobic sludge) was injected into the MFCs anode chamber in the N2 atmosphere, and the potassium ferricyanide solution was injected into the MFCs cathode chamber. Then, the voltage of MFCs was collected using a multi-channel voltage recorder. The acetate solution was replaced with a new solution after the voltage of the MFCs decreased below 50 mV. The as-prepared samples and raw CC were fixed to the mixed bacterial culture MFC devices and were run by external resistance of 1000 Ω. As indicated in Fig. 5 a, the output voltage of the MFC with Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs anode increased quickly to a stable shock region of 569 ± 0.05 mV in a short time, suggesting a quick active bacterial adhesion. After a stable cell voltage, a slow decrease in voltage is observed due to the depletion of acetate. After replenishing with the new anodic and cathodic solutions, the voltage recovered quickly to a notably high level. In the case of CC demonstrated in Fig. 5a, the maximum cell voltage, approximately 480 ± 0.03 mV, was achieved that is lower compared to Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs. With the knowledge above, the MFC arrayed with Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs generated more power from the breakdown (depleted) of the identical mass of added acetate. Hence, this merit Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs was imputed to the large surface area and increased the produced electrons between the biofilm and the electrode due to creating a Fe@Fe2O3 nanoparticles smart layer that enhanced microorganisms’ adhesion.

Polarization curves represent a powerful tool for the analysis and characterization of fuel cells. A power density curve that describes the power as the function of the current density is calculated from the polarization curve. After multiple cycles, power output and polarization profiles were assayed with a series of resistors (100–0.1 KΩ). As can be seen in Fig. 5c, compared to the CC, the cell voltage with Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs was reduced rather slowly by diminishing external loading, suggesting minimum polarization which means a much smaller internal resistance owing to the quick charge transfer kinetics and excellent conductivity of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs. Furthermore, Fig. 5b illustrated that the maximum power density and current density produced by the MFCs increase in the order of the mentioned anodes: CC (250 ± 20 mW/m2 and 0.66 ± 0.03 A/m2), NCQDs (446 ± 11 mW/m2 and 1.26 ± 0.02 A/m2) and Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs (836 ± 8 mW/m2 and 2.63 ± 0.02 A/m2). Compared with some closely related (but not all) the anodes recently reported in the literature, the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs anode exhibits MFC with larger power density (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of EET process for different anodes
ce:section.ce:para: CV studies of substrate depletion (non-turnover) and maximum voltage output (turnover) steps were accomplished to further explore the EET process from the microorganism to the underneath electrode. Commonly, valuable witting on these two separate EET procedures can be gained via CV test, involving indirect electron transfer via mobile redox shuttles and direct electron transfer through outer-membrane c-type cytochromes [41,42]. As shown in Fig. 6 a (inset); CC, NCQDs and Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs electrodes did not show any activity before inoculation (no obvious redox peaks) toward the oxidation of acetate. As can be seen in Fig. 6b, the scans reveal a symmetrical redox couple for NCQDs and Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs, corresponding to the catalytic oxidation of substrate by as-papered anodes [42]. In comparison, just a cathodic peak could be observed on a CC in the inoculated medium. The maximum outward current density for the biofilm of the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs (2.42 mA/cm2) is far higher than that of NCQDs (0.35 mA/cm2) and CC (0.10 mA/cm2) anodes. The increase in current density is indicative of the contribution of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs in CC. After substrate depletion, the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs anode unfolds two major redox pairs: a weak anodic peak at −0.22 V corresponding to cathodic peak at −0.30 and an anodic peak at −0.31 V corresponding to a cathodic peak at −0.41 V. These two redox pairs are similar in shape to those of other electroactive biofilms which could be attributed to the redox of outer membrane cytochromes (OMCs) [43–45]. Moreover, the anodic potential of 0.14 V observed in the voltammogram may correspond to originate from some unidentified bacterial metabolites [46]. The highest redox peak current intensity of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs implies the synergetic advantages of Fe@Fe2O3 and NCQDs and more redox-active species in the biofilm.

The electron transfer kinetics has been described in the form of the Butler-Volmer (B-V) equation. When η is small, the i 0 value is dependent on the electrochemical process without considering the effect of mass transfer. Under the low overpotential, the B-V kinetics takes into account only the activation parameters (η and transfer coefficient). By choosing this low overpotential range, each value of exchange current density, i 0, is calculated by Tafel plot. To clarify the roles of the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs in the bioelectrocatalytic activity and EET efficiency, Tafel plot analysis was conducted. Fig. 7 displays the Tafel region for the MFCs equipped with various anodes. The value of the b a (Tafel slope) and the i 0 (exchange current density) were ordered as follows: Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs (0.018 V/dec, 1.52 × 10−5 A/cm2), NCQDs (0.026 V/dec, 4.22 × 10−6 A/cm2) and CC (0.031 V/dec, 1.14 × 10−6 A/cm2), respectively. Consequently, the lower b a and higher i 0 prove greater electron discharge capability and faster reaction rate of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs.

To verify that the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs surface has suitable biocompatibility, the reasonable adhesion of its microbes was immediately examined by FESEM. The FESEM images display the grown biofilm on skeletons of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs, NCQDs and CC anodes after a one-month discharge (Fig. 8 ). Clearly visible is that the fabricated Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs anode (Fig. 8a) illustrated more intense bacterial communities on the surface of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs in comparison with the NCQDs and CC anode (Fig. 8b,c). The plain surface of the CC anode was averse to the microorganism adhesion, giving rise to fewer bacterial colonies to generate electrons. In this regard, the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs provides green and sustainable energy device with improved power output by giving microbes a chance to attach to and move on anodes and accelerating the charge transfer between anode and bacteria. With the results available, the electrocatalysis mechanism of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs can be illustrated schematically in Fig. 8d.

Based on this study, a high-performance and low-cost multicomponent hybrid material was fabricated using a facile electrodeposition technique. Of particular interest is the fact that Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs promotes electron transfer and lead to high MFC performance. Fe@Fe2O3 renders superb biocompatibility with electroactive microorganisms; exclusively its capability to strongly adhere to electroactive bacteria on substrate and N-doping improves the electronic conductivity of carbon quantum dots. With these contributions, the MFC equipped with the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs anode attained a power density of 836 ± 8 mW/m2 and a current density of 2.63 ± 0.02 mA/m2 with reliable stability, which excels the pristine CC anode. Worth mentioning, the electrochemical analysis displayed that the Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs possessed higher peak current density ascribed to OMCs and an extremely lower charge transfer resistance. Finally, the demonstration proved that the premiere performance of Fe@Fe2O3/NCQDs can be attributed to the lower internal resistance, accelerated extracellular and higher kinetics of electrochemical reaction resulting from the synergetic advantages of the Fe@Fe2O3 and NCQDs.
Maryam Farahmand Habibi: Data curation, Writing, Original draft preparation. Majid Arvand: Supervision, Methodology, Validation, Reviewing and Editing. Shabnam Sohrabnezhad: Methodology, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are highly grateful for the financial support from the Iranian National Science Foundation and post-graduate office of Guilan University.
